title: Quick check: the four product surfaces

questions.prompt: Managed ITDR covers which identity systems?

choices.label: Microsoft 365 only.
choices.rationale: True before the ITDR rename; not true today. ITDR broadened the old M365-only product to also cover Google Workspace.

choices: Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace, in a single surface.

choices.label: Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and Okta.
choices.rationale: Okta isn't named in the lesson's enumeration. Don't invent surfaces that aren't there.

choices.label: On-prem Active Directory and Azure AD.
choices.rationale: ITDR covers M365 and GWS at the SaaS-identity layer; the lesson doesn't list on-prem AD.

questions.explanation: ITDR is the rename of the old MDR for Microsoft 365 product, broadened to also cover Google Workspace.

questions.prompt: A peer says: "ITDR is just for Microsoft 365." Why is that misconception worth correcting now?
choices: Because the peer may bounce Google Workspace tickets back as "wrong product," delaying the response.

choices.label: Because Google Workspace customers pay more and shouldn't be neglected.
choices.rationale: Pricing isn't the reason. Workflow correctness is.

choices.label: Because Microsoft 365 isn't actually covered by ITDR anymore.
choices.rationale: M365 is still covered; ITDR is a broadening, not a switch away.

choices.label: Because the rename means the old M365 features have been removed.
choices.rationale: The rename added scope (GWS), it didn't remove M365 coverage.

questions.explanation: The misconception causes real workflow harm — bouncing GWS tickets as wrong product.

questions.prompt: Ransomware canaries — what severity do their trips carry by default, and why?

choices.label: Low by default; canary trips happen frequently and are usually false positives.
choices.rationale: Wrong on both facts. Canaries are placed where normal users won't touch them.

choices.label: High by default; the SOC reviews each trip and decides whether to escalate further.
choices.rationale: Wrong severity.

choices: Critical by default; the assumption is that the only thing tripping a canary is something the customer needs contained now.

choices.label: Critical by default; canaries are a separate subscription with their own SLA.
choices.rationale: Half right on severity, wrong on the product model. Canaries ride under EDR.

questions.explanation: Canary trips are Critical by default — the trip itself implies real ransomware activity.

questions.prompt: A new tech says: "Canaries are a separate Huntress product I have to learn separately." What's the right correction?
choices: They're a tripwire under EDR — same agent, same SOC, a sharper incident class.

choices.label: They're a separate subscription the MSP may or may not have bought.
choices.rationale: Wrong commercial model. Canaries come with EDR.

choices.label: They're an ITDR feature for catching identity-based ransomware.
choices.rationale: Wrong surface. Canaries are decoy files on endpoints.

choices.label: They were retired in 2024 and you'll never see them.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts.

questions.explanation: Canaries are a tripwire mechanism delivered alongside Managed EDR, not a separate product.

questions.prompt: Managed SIEM — what's the right framing for a tech whose MSP doesn't subscribe to it?

choices.label: SIEM is dead to you; skip those lessons forever.
choices.rationale: Treats "optional" as "deleted." If your MSP adds the product later, those lessons re-apply.

choices: Optional today; if your MSP adds the product later, the lessons re-apply. Don't treat optional as deleted.

choices.label: Read the SIEM lessons anyway; SIEM might land on your queue without warning.
choices.rationale: If the MSP doesn't subscribe, SIEM incidents don't land. Relearn when the product arrives.

choices.label: Skip SIEM and treat it as ITDR — they're the same thing.
choices.rationale: ITDR is identity-side telemetry; SIEM is log ingestion. Not interchangeable.

questions.explanation: Optional today doesn't mean deleted forever.

questions.prompt: Three incidents land in your queue inside ten minutes: (1) Suspicious sign-in, alice@example.com, impossible travel detected. (2) Process detection, SRV-FILE01, powershell.exe invoked via scheduled task. (3) Canary tripped, WS-FINANCE-04, files modified on protected paths. Which surface produced each?

choices.label: All three are EDR, since all involve customer assets.
choices.rationale: Wrong. #1 is identity (UPN + sign-in = ITDR). #3 is a canary trip — under EDR but sharper.

choices: 1 is ITDR, 2 is EDR, 3 is a canary trip (under EDR, Critical by default).

choices.label: 1 is ITDR, 2 is SIEM, 3 is EDR.
choices.rationale: Mistakes a scheduled-task process detection (agent-collected behavioural signal) for a SIEM log event.

choices.label: 1 is SIEM, 2 is EDR, 3 is ITDR.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the first and third. UPN + sign-in is ITDR; canary trip is under EDR.

questions.explanation: UPN vs. hostname is the easiest tell; canary names itself.

questions.prompt: Same three incidents — an ITDR impossible-travel signal, an EDR scheduled-task PowerShell detection, and a ransomware canary trip. Which do you reach for first?

choices.label: The ITDR sign-in — identity has the biggest blast radius.
choices.rationale: Reasonable instinct but incomplete. A canary trip is Critical by default; Critical jumps the queue.

choices: The canary trip first, then the identity incident.

choices.label: The PowerShell scheduled task — most concrete signal.
choices.rationale: Bread-and-butter EDR work; can wait while Critical and high-blast-radius events clear first.

choices.label: Work them in the order they arrived.
choices.rationale: Arrival order isn't the prioritisation rule. Severity is.

questions.explanation: Canary trips are Critical until proven otherwise — containment-first response beats high blast radius for tempo.

questions.prompt: Canaries don't get their own top-level item in the portal. Where do canary trips show up?

choices.label: In a dedicated "Canaries" section under Settings.
choices.rationale: Canaries don't have a top-level item.

choices: In the Incidents list, tagged as Critical with a canary indicator on the report.

choices.label: Only as an email; they don't appear in the portal.
choices.rationale: Wrong. They appear in Incidents with a tag.

choices.label: Under the Agents view, attached to the protected host's record.
choices.rationale: Canary trips surface as incidents, not as agent properties.

questions.explanation: Surfaced through the standard Incidents list with a tag, not a separate navigation item.

questions.prompt: A client asks: "Can you set up the phishing-simulation campaigns we talked about?" Which Huntress product does this map to?
choices: Managed SAT. Not covered in this course; if your MSP runs it, hand the request to whoever owns SAT internally.

choices.label: Managed ITDR. Phishing simulations are an identity surface in 2026.
choices.rationale: ITDR watches real identity signals, not simulations.

choices.label: Managed EDR. The agent runs the simulations from the endpoint.
choices.rationale: EDR collects endpoint telemetry; it doesn't drive phishing simulations.

choices.label: Managed SIEM, by ingesting click-tracking logs.
choices.rationale: SIEM ingests logs; running simulations is a different workflow.

questions.explanation: SAT is deliberately out of scope for this course and has its own portal areas and a separate course at MSPs that run it.

questions.prompt: You're reading an older Huntress doc that mentions "MDR for Microsoft 365." Which of these is true today?

choices.label: It's a discontinued product no longer available.
choices.rationale: It was renamed and broadened, not retired.

choices.label: It's the current name for ransomware canaries.
choices.rationale: Canaries are an EDR tripwire; the old name was the identity surface.

choices: It's the pre-rename name for ITDR; the current product covers both M365 and Google Workspace.

choices.label: It's the pre-rename name for Managed SIEM.
choices.rationale: SIEM launched April 2025 as a new product.

questions.explanation: Same product, broader scope. ITDR is the post-rename, M365-plus-GWS version.
